glossary.definition: Trust or something entrusted to one's care. In Islamic finance, it refers to the concept that wealth and resources are held in trust from Allah, and must be managed responsibly.

A sale contract where the seller sells an asset to the buyer on deferred payment, then buys it back at a lower cash price. This is controversial and considered impermissible by many scholars as it resembles interest-based lending.
Success and prosperity in both this world and the hereafter. Islamic economics aims to achieve Falah for individuals and society.

Excessive uncertainty or ambiguity in a contract. Islamic finance prohibits transactions with excessive Gharar to ensure fairness and transparency. Minor Gharar that doesn't affect the contract is tolerable.
Permissible or lawful according to Islamic law. In finance, it refers to investments, products, and activities that comply with Sharia principles.
Prohibited or unlawful according to Islamic law. In finance, it includes interest (Riba), gambling (Maysir), and investments in prohibited industries (alcohol, pork, etc.).

Leasing or hiring contract where the owner transfers the usufruct (right to use) of an asset to another person for a specified period and rental payment. Common in Islamic banking for equipment and vehicle financing.
A lease agreement with an option to purchase. The lessee pays rent and eventually acquires ownership of the asset, similar to a lease-to-own arrangement.
Juristic preference or equity. A method of Islamic jurisprudence where a jurist departs from a stricter ruling to a more equitable one based on stronger evidence or public interest.
Trusteeship or vicegerency. The concept that humans are stewards of Allah's creation and will be accountable for how they manage resources and responsibilities.
The objectives or goals of Islamic law. These include protection of faith (Din), life (Nasl), intellect (Aql), wealth (Mal), and dignity (Ird). Islamic finance aims to serve these objectives.
Public interest or welfare. A principle in Islamic jurisprudence that considers the broader benefit to society when formulating rules and policies.
Gambling or games of chance. Prohibited in Islam because it involves gaining wealth through chance rather than productive effort, creating wealth redistribution without real economic activity.

A partnership contract where one partner provides capital (Rabb al-Mal) and the other provides labor and management (Mudarib). Profits are shared according to a pre-agreed ratio, while losses are borne by the capital provider unless due to negligence.
Cost-plus financing where the seller discloses the actual cost of an asset and adds a known profit margin. The buyer pays in installments or lump sum. It's the most common mode of Islamic financing.
A partnership where all partners contribute capital and may share in management. Profits are distributed according to an agreed ratio, while losses are shared in proportion to capital contribution.
A benevolent or interest-free loan. The borrower is only required to return the principal amount. It is considered a charitable act and highly rewarded in Islam.
Interest or usury. Any predetermined, fixed return on a loan regardless of the borrower's profit or loss. It is strictly prohibited in Islam as it is considered unjust and exploitative.

Islamic law derived from the Quran, Sunnah (traditions of Prophet Muhammad), Ijma (scholarly consensus), and Qiyas (analogical reasoning). It governs all aspects of Muslim life including financial transactions.

Islamic financial certificates representing undivided ownership shares in tangible assets, usufruct, or services. Often called Islamic bonds, they differ from conventional bonds as they represent ownership rather than debt.
Islamic cooperative insurance based on mutual assistance. Participants contribute to a shared fund as donations. Claims are paid from the pool, and surpluses may be distributed among participants.
The practice of purchasing an asset on credit and selling it to a third party for cash to obtain liquidity. It is considered permissible by most scholars as it involves real buying and selling.
An Islamic endowment where property or assets are permanently dedicated for charitable purposes. The asset cannot be sold, inherited, or donated, and its usufruct is used for specified purposes.
Obligatory charity, one of the Five Pillars of Islam. Muslims must give 2.5% of their qualifying wealth annually to eligible recipients. It purifies wealth and helps those in need.
